---
title: "Nuclear Reactor Cooling Timeline 2: The Legend of Curly's Gold"
format: html
jupyter: python3
highlight-style: default
code-block-bg: "#000000"
filters:
    - shinylive
---
# Modeling Report (Taken from MATH 283 Unit 3 Project)
## Corey Silver
### Scenario: Cooling Performance During Reactor Shutdown

Engineers are studying how quickly reactor components cool following shutdown.

# 1. Measurements:

| Time after Shutdown ($\text{hr}$) | Temperature $(^\circ C)$|
|---------|:-----|
| 1 | 580 |
| 2 | 510 | 
| 4 | 430 |
| 8 | 340 |
|12 | 290 |
|18 | 230 |

```{python}

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np
from htmltools.svg import title
import scipy as sci
import pandas as pd
import IPython.display as ipd
from shinylive import *

from error_analysis import *
from plots_and_tables import *
from fitting_functions import *


X = [1, 2, 4, 8, 12, 18]
# See section 3 for explanation of modification of T data.
Y = [1.05*y for y in [580, 510, 430, 340, 290, 230]]
N=3
```

"A large prediction error could affect cooling procedures and operational decisions.

Your team has been asked to recommend the best model and fitting strategy."


# 2. Objective Definition

We need to reliably predict the temperature of the reactor (in degrees Centigrade) from the time passed since shutoff (in hours). Since this is a live nuclear reactor, criticality is a prime safety concern.


# 3. Assumptions and Simplifications

In any mathematical model, there are certain assumptions made and simplifications accepted. For this model, we keep in mind the sensitive nature of the system in question, which leades us to an overarching principle which we'll follow throughout this modelling process.

## Our first and central assumption is that of the *worst-case scenario*: we assume the least optimal conditions, in every case, for time to cooling. For example, when we assume a constant external temperature, we assume that temperature to be the mean daily maximum for the season in which we operate. We assume high humidity that will slow the cooling rate for the reactor components, etc.

1. Bulk, uniform cooling rate: no regions of greater $T$ density
1. Single substance component: single specific heat
1. Constant external temperature
1. Constant external humidity, pressure: We assume no weather.
1. We assume the absence of other heat sources, ignoring the effect of sunlight.
1. Reactor isn't operated until cooling is complete
1. We assume a $-5{\%}$ margin of error with temperature measurement (The observed temeperatures have been increased by a factor of $1.05$). This will be one in a group of safety redundancies. 

# 4. Variables and Parameters

$T_R$ is the reactor temperature.

$T_E$ is the external temperature

$t$ is the time from shutdown in hours.

That's it! While a high-fidelity model would and could account for the specific heats of individual reactor components, the role of humidity in cooling, and the stochastic nature of thermodynamic processes, we can simplify extensively for the purpose of this problem. Furthermore, we're not supplied with external temperature data. Therefore, we'll leave out the external temperature as an explicit variable and **we'll model the cooling rate exclusively in terms of the reactor temperature and time passed.** 

First, we're supplied with cooling and time data; this means that we must assume similar external conditions throughout the cooling period in order to use this supplied data for interpolation and limited extrapolation. Second, we can justify some simplification from the safety-focused nature of the model we're designing. We do not know the external conditions for the data we're given, so the most prudent approach is to assume that these conditions are favorable to quick cooling and consider them optimized.  We will start, at least, from a basic model of heat transfer. This is most appropriate for the limited data we have on the situation.

# 5. Model Development
We'll test optimized Chebyshev, Least-Squares, and Absolute-Deviation fits of the following models: 

## Linear:

Of form $T(t)=c_1\cdot t + c_2$.

```{python}

plotter(X, Y, "linear", 1)
coeff_table(X, Y, "linear", 1)
```

## Polynomial: 

Of form $T(t)=c_1t^n + c_2t^{n-1} +...+c_kt^0$. 

We'll limit $n$ and $k$ to four or five, at most. Any higher and at that point we'd be overfitting to approximate a power curve. Overfitting can introduce enormous spikes in a polynomial fit due to the Gibbs Phenomenon (I think). Please use the slider below to observe the effect of polynomial degree on fit parameters and optimization quality.

```{shinylive-python}
#| standalone: true
#| viewerHeight: 500

"""
code modified from example in docs at https://quarto-ext.github.io/shinylive/
This gemini conversation used for debugging and editing:                                              
https://gemini.google.com/share/59ac2467bf7b                                  
"""
from shiny import *
import shinywidgets as shw

from main import *
from fitting_functions import *
from error_analysis import *
from plots_and_tables import *

app_ui = ui.page_fluid(
    ui.layout_columns(
        ui.div(
            ui.card(
                ui.h4("Poly-fitter"),
                ui.input_slider("n", "Degree of Polynomial Fit:", min=1, max=20, value=2),
                bg="#C0C0C0"
        ),

            ui.card(
                ui.card_header("Fit Parameters"),
                ui.output_table("coefficients"),
                style="overflow-x: auto;"  # Keeps wide tables behaving cleanly
        )
        ),
        # Visual Card for the Plot
        ui.card(
            ui.card_header("Optimization-type Comparison"),
            ui.output_plot("conspiracy"),
            full_screen=True  # Adds a neat zoom button for the user
        ),
        
        # Visual Card for the Data Table
        col_widths=[7, 5],  # Allocates 7/12ths of width to plot, 5/12ths to table
title = "Degree Comparison for Polynomial Fits")
)

def server(input: Inputs, output: Outputs, session: Session):
    @render.plot
    def conspiracy():
        poly_deg = input.n()
        return plotter(X, Y, "polynomial", poly_deg)
    @render.table
    def coefficients():
        poly_deg = input.n()
        return coeff_table(X, Y, "polynomial", poly_deg)




## file: main.py
{{< include main.py >}}

## file: fitting_functions.py
{{< include fitting_functions.py >}}

## file: plots_and_tables.py
{{< include plots_and_tables.py >}}

## file: error_analyzer.py
{{< include error_analysis.py >}}

## file: requirements.txt
matplotlib
numpy
scipy
pandas
IPython
```

## Exponential decay:

Of form $T(t) = (c_2)e^{c_1t}$ ($c_2$ is negative).

We'll linearize this for two reasons; so that this fit can be optimized with linear programming and for the sake of visual clarity. Taking the natural log of both sides gives us

$$
\ln[T(t)]=c_1t+c_2.
$$

Simple inspection of the plots makes it look as though the exponential model fits the data most closely. We cannot confirm this hunch, however, until we do some analysis on the error of the various plots.
```{python}

plotter(X, Y, "exponential", 1)
coeff_table(X, Y, "exponential", 1)
```

## Power model:

Of form $T(t) = (c_2)^{c_1t}$

We'll linearize this sort of model in the same way, also giving us what looks like a strong candidate for a predictive and interpolative fit.

$$
\ln[T(t)]=c_1t+c_2.
$$

```{python}

poly_deg = 1

plotter(X, Y, "power", 1)
coeff_table(X, Y, "power", 1)
```

# 6. Interpretation

Results of error analysis are given in the table below.

```{shinylive-python}
#| standalone: true
#| viewerHeight: 800

"""
code modified from example in docs at https://quarto-ext.github.io/shinylive/
This gemini conversation used for debugging and editing:                                              
https://gemini.google.com/share/59ac2467bf7b                                  
"""

from main import *
from fitting_functions import *
from error_analysis import *
from plots_and_tables import *
from shiny import *
import shinywidgets as shw

app_ui = ui.page_fluid(
    ui.layout_columns(
        ui.div(
        ui.input_slider("n", "Degree of Polynomial Fit:", min=1, max=20, value = 2),
        ui.output_table("error_table") 
    )
)
)

def server(input: Inputs, output: Outputs, session: Session):
    """
    @render.data_frame
    def error_table():
        poly_deg = input.n()
        return main(X, Y, poly_deg)
    """
    
    @render.table
    def error_table():
        poly_deg = input.n()
        return main(X, Y, n=poly_deg)
          

app = App(app_ui, server)

## file: main.py
{{< include main.py >}}

## file: fitting_functions.py
{{< include fitting_functions.py >}}

## file: plots_and_tables.py
{{< include plots_and_tables.py >}}

## file: error_analyzer.py
{{< include error_analysis.py >}}

## file: requirements.txt
matplotlib
numpy
scipy
pandas
tabulate
```

As can be seen in the table above, naïve observation looks to be correct, here. The exponential cooling model shows a much lower error across the dataset than do any of the polynomial models. Our model requires a focus on safety, so we'll use a Chebyshev optimization of the exponential fit. See the following section for a more thorough explanation of these choices.

Since the exponential model shows the lowest error, we'll use it for extrapolation. We make a prediction for the bulk temperature of the reactor components after $24$ hours of cooling.
```{python}

temp_finder(X, Y, N)
```

# 7. Validation

We chose a Chebyshev error-optimized exponential fit to model the cooling rate of this nuclear plant reactor.
This is because the worst case scenario (the largest single residual) is minimized through Chebyshev optimization. We need to account not just for a comprehensive overview of error, but for the Murphy's Law example where the air is hot and humid, the cooling pool circulator is broken, and all other systems operate at minimal effectiveness. We must work under these assumptions because an overly optimistic prediction could have unacceptable consequences to the plant and its environs. We chose the exponential fit because it minimized the Chebyshev error most effectively and did so with the least over-fitting of any model.
This model for the cooling rate is backed up empirically. It's a barely-modified form of Newton's Law of cooling:
$$
    T(t) = T_R + (T_E - T_R)e^{-kt}
$$
We are only concerned with the medium, that is the reactor itself, so we don't include the $T(t)$ term, instead letting the reactor temperature take its place.

\begin{aligned}
T_R(t) & = (T_E - T_R)e^{-kt} \\
 \ln(T_R(t)) & = \ln[(T_E-T_R)e^{-kt}] \\
 \ln(T_R(t)) & = \ln(T_E - T_R) & - kt \\
 \ln(T_R(t)) & = c_1 & -c_0t \\
\end{aligned}

Where $T_R$ is the reactor temperature and $T_E$ is the external temperature, substituted for $T_0$ due to our assumption that the external/surrounding air temperature is constant across the range of observation.


# 8. Re-evaluation and Improvement

This model has a major weakness in that it approaches the problem deterministically. In reality, we would benefit from introducing randomness into our analysis, since that could provide closer estimates of daily temperature, humidity, and component failure rates while retaining this model's emphasis on multi-system error. That would enable a more efficient response to uncertainty than the chosen model where all predictions are made according to an edge case.

Additionally, we assumed bulk cooling, here. Further consideration of this problem as a stochastic process would involve the treatment of heat density within the reactor. This would allow for a more accurate thermodynamical analysis and enable response to uneven coooling rates; we should be equally concerned with a single region of retained heat as with a higher temperature across the bulk. This more accurate treatment would involve solving the PDE version of the heat equation

$$
\frac{\partial u}{\partial t} = k \nabla^2 u + \frac{Q(x, t)}{c\rho}
$$

And using what data we have to narrow down the resultant Fourier series family of solutions into a range of particular solutions. For the scope of the problem as presented, a simpler solution is valid as long as it overcorrects enough to preserve safety requirements.

# Bibliography

1. "4.2A Newton's Law of Cooling." Ximera, The Ohio State University, ximera.osu.edu/ode/main/lawOfCooling/lawOfCooling. Accessed 15 June 2026.
1. Dawkins, Paul. "The Heat Equation." Paul's Online Math Notes: Differential Equations, Lamar University, tutorial.math.lamar.edu/Classes/DE/TheHeatEquation.aspx. Accessed 19 June 2026.
